• Show All Code
  • Hide All Code

Within-Visit Assay Correlations and Maternal Vaccine-Responder Subgroups

Within-subject correlation of 6 assay responses at three serum timepoints; detection of low/high universal maternal responders across TdaP vs TT

Scot Zens and Anja Saso

2026-07-10

Purpose

This module answers two families of questions, both anchored to the same maternal/cord/infant serology dataset used by the PTNA, SBA and WT_IgG endpoint analyses and by the 06_prediction_models module:

A. Within-subject correlation (Q1-Q5) of the six assay responses (WHOLE_PTNA, WHOLE_SBA, WHOLE_WT_IgG, PRN_IgG, FIM_IgG, PT_IgG) at each of the five serum draws – PregEarly and MatBirth (maternal), then InfMon2, InfMon5 and InfMon9 (infant). The maternal draws carry only the three antigen-specific totals; the full six-assay structure appears at the three infant visits.

B. Staged responder subgrouping (Q6) using two timepoint sets, defined independently and then linked, so the maternal signal and the infant signal are never conflated:

  1. Stage A - maternal response (up to MatBirth). Low vs high maternal responders to vaccination in pregnancy, from the maternal binding panel at PregEarly + MatBirth.
  2. Stage B - infant chain (baseline -> responses). Low vs high infant responders across InfMon2 -> InfMon5 -> InfMon9, where InfMon2 is the transplacental baseline (pre-primary) and InfMon5/InfMon9 are the post-priming responses. This set adds the whole-bacterial functional assays, which exist only at infant visits.
  3. Stage C - linkage. Does maternal responder status carry forward – first into the infant baseline, then into the infant’s own post-vaccination responses? We correlate the two scores, cross-tabulate the two subgroupings, and trace the infant trajectory (InfMon2 -> InfMon5 -> InfMon9) stratified by maternal subgroup. Arm concordance (TdaP vs TT) is tested at each stage.

Scale caveat (carried from cord_to_infmon2_analysis). Maternal and cord samples were assayed on different plates/runs from the infant samples, so cross-timepoint comparisons require the cross-plate-normalised scale. Every correlation here (Q1-Q5) is computed within a single timepoint, so the per-plate offset is a constant that cancels and does not bias a within-visit correlation. The staged subgrouping (Q6) likewise standardises every feature to a within-timepoint rank / z-score before any cross-visit combination, so the maternal panel, the infant panel and the InfMon2 -> InfMon5 -> InfMon9 trajectory are each on their own per-timepoint scale and the offset never enters.

Assay availability (from config/endpoints.R). The whole-bacterial endpoints are not assayed on maternal serum. WHOLE_PTNA and WHOLE_SBA exist only at InfMon2, InfMon5 and InfMon9; WHOLE_WT_IgG exists at those three plus CordBlood. None of the three is measured at PregEarly or MatBirth. The three antigen-specific totals (PT_IgG, PRN_IgG, FIM_IgG) do exist at every visit. Consequently the early-pregnancy and at-birth matrices (Q1, Q2) contain only those three antigen totals, and the full six-assay matrix is populated only at InfMon2 (Q3). These are by-design absences, surfaced in Table 1, not coding errors.

Coverage of the six assays at each timepoint

The whole-bacterial functional/binding assays (WHOLE_PTNA, WHOLE_SBA, WHOLE_WT_IgG) are run on infant serum only – PTNA and SBA at InfMon2/5/9, and WT_IgG at those plus CordBlood – so they are absent at the early-pregnancy and at-birth maternal draws by design. The three antigen-specific totals are present throughout. We audit this first so any empty correlation cell is attributable to the assay schedule, not to a coding error.

## status() distinguishes three reasons a cell can be empty:
##   - absent (not in panel)           : feature string not in the data at all
##   - not measured here (by design)   : a WHOLE_* assay at a visit where it is
##                                       not run (maternal draws) -- per config
##   - present                         : reports the n with a finite value
cov_tbl <- lapply(names(wide_by_visit), function(tp) {
  visit <- TIMEPOINTS[[tp]]
  w <- wide_by_visit[[tp]]
  data.frame(
    timepoint = tp,
    assay     = params$assays,
    resolved  = vapply(params$assays, function(a) {
      r <- rf(a); if (is.na(r)) "absent (not in panel)" else r }, character(1)),
    status = vapply(params$assays, function(a) {
      r <- rf(a)
      if (is.na(r))                                   "absent (not in panel)"
      else if (!feature_measured_at(r, visit, WHOLE_MAP)) "not measured here (by design)"
      else                                            "present"
    }, character(1)),
    n_nonmissing = vapply(params$assays, function(a) {
      r <- rf(a)
      if (is.na(r) || !(r %in% names(w))) 0L else sum(is.finite(w[[r]]))
    }, integer(1)),
    row.names = NULL)
}) %>% dplyr::bind_rows()

for (i in seq_len(nrow(cov_tbl)))
  nt_log_manual(nt, "WithinVisitCor", cov_tbl$timepoint[i], cov_tbl$assay[i],
                "assay coverage", n_entered = NA, n_used = cov_tbl$n_nonmissing[i],
                note = cov_tbl$status[i])

knitr::kable(cov_tbl,
  caption = "Table 1. Coverage of the six assays at each serum timepoint. WHOLE_PTNA / WHOLE_SBA are measured only at InfMon2/5/9 and WHOLE_WT_IgG additionally at CordBlood, so all three read 'not measured here (by design)' at the maternal draws.")
Table 1. Coverage of the six assays at each serum timepoint. WHOLE_PTNA / WHOLE_SBA are measured only at InfMon2/5/9 and WHOLE_WT_IgG additionally at CordBlood, so all three read ‘not measured here (by design)’ at the maternal draws.
timepoint assay resolved status n_nonmissing

Early pregnancy (maternal) WHOLE_WT_IgG WHOLE_WT_IgG not measured here (by design) 0

Early pregnancy (maternal) FIM_IgG FIM_IgG present 312
Early pregnancy (maternal) PT_IgG PT_IgG present 312

At birth (maternal) WHOLE_SBA WHOLE_SBA not measured here (by design) 0
At birth (maternal) WHOLE_WT_IgG WHOLE_WT_IgG not measured here (by design) 0

At birth (maternal) FIM_IgG FIM_IgG present 283
At birth (maternal) PT_IgG PT_IgG present 283
InfMon2 (infant baseline) WHOLE_PTNA WHOLE_PTNA present 237
InfMon2 (infant baseline) WHOLE_SBA WHOLE_SBA present 222
InfMon2 (infant baseline) WHOLE_WT_IgG WHOLE_WT_IgG present 223
InfMon2 (infant baseline) PRN_IgG PRN_IgG present 287
InfMon2 (infant baseline) FIM_IgG FIM_IgG present 287
InfMon2 (infant baseline) PT_IgG PT_IgG present 287
InfMon5 (infant) WHOLE_PTNA WHOLE_PTNA present 236
InfMon5 (infant) WHOLE_SBA WHOLE_SBA present 229
InfMon5 (infant) WHOLE_WT_IgG WHOLE_WT_IgG present 221
InfMon5 (infant) PRN_IgG PRN_IgG present 281
InfMon5 (infant) FIM_IgG FIM_IgG present 281
InfMon5 (infant) PT_IgG PT_IgG present 281
InfMon9 (infant) WHOLE_PTNA WHOLE_PTNA present 236
InfMon9 (infant) WHOLE_SBA WHOLE_SBA present 209
InfMon9 (infant) WHOLE_WT_IgG WHOLE_WT_IgG present 222
InfMon9 (infant) PRN_IgG PRN_IgG present 306
InfMon9 (infant) FIM_IgG FIM_IgG present 306
InfMon9 (infant) PT_IgG PT_IgG present 306

Q1-Q5 - Within-subject correlations at each timepoint

The correlation engine, its long-format flattener, and the heatmap renderer all live in R/within_visit_helpers.R (within_visit_cor(), cor_long(), plot_cor()). They use pairwise-complete observations, report the n behind every cell, and BH-adjust p-values across the unique assay pairs.

cor_results        <- list()
cor_strata_results <- list()

if (!is.na(infant_arm_src))
  cat(sprintf("*Infant arm (wP vs aP) resolved from `%s`; used to stratify InfMon5/InfMon9 below.*\n\n",
              infant_arm_src))

Infant arm (wP vs aP) resolved from data_raw$infant_arm; used to stratify InfMon5/InfMon9 below.

for (tp in names(wide_by_visit)) {
  w_tp <- wide_by_visit[[tp]]
  cc   <- within_visit_cor(w_tp, ASSAYS, method = params$cor_method)
  cor_results[[tp]] <- cc

  cat("\n\n## ", tp, "\n\n")
  cat(sprintf("Method: **%s**; pairwise-complete observations; p-values BH-adjusted across the %d unique assay pairs.\n\n",
              cc$method, choose(length(cc$cols), 2)))

  cat("**Correlation coefficients (r) -- all infants:**\n\n")
  print(knitr::kable(round(cc$r, 2), caption = sprintf("r matrix - %s (all infants)", tp)))
  cat("\n\n**Pairwise n behind each cell:**\n\n")
  print(knitr::kable(cc$n, caption = sprintf("n matrix - %s", tp)))
  cat("\n\n")

  plot_cor(cc, sprintf("Within-subject correlation - %s (all infants)", tp), has_ggcorr)
  cat("\n\n")

  ## ---- Q4/Q5: stratify InfMon5 & InfMon9 by infant arm (wP vs aP) ----------
  ## The combined ("all infants") matrix above is always kept; the two arm
  ## matrices and a colour-coded scatter panel are added alongside it.
  if (tp %in% STRATIFY_TPS) {
    w_arm <- attach_infant_arm(w_tp, infant_arm_tbl)
    if (!is.null(infant_arm_tbl) && any(!is.na(w_arm$infant_arm))) {
      sc <- within_visit_cor_strata(w_arm, ASSAYS, "infant_arm",
                                    method = params$cor_method)
      cor_strata_results[[tp]] <- sc

      for (g in names(sc$by)) {
        ng <- sum(!is.na(w_arm$infant_arm) & w_arm$infant_arm == g)
        cat(sprintf("\n\n**Correlation coefficients (r) -- %s infants (n = %d):**\n\n", g, ng))
        print(knitr::kable(round(sc$by[[g]]$r, 2),
              caption = sprintf("r matrix - %s (%s infants)", tp, g)))
        cat("\n\n")
        plot_cor(sc$by[[g]],
                 sprintf("Within-subject correlation - %s (%s)", tp, g), has_ggcorr)
        cat("\n\n")
      }

      cat(sprintf("\n\n**Scatter panel -- %s.** Points are subjects coloured by infant arm; coloured lines are per-arm OLS fits, the dashed black line is the overall (combined) fit. Each facet is annotated with the overall and per-arm Spearman r, matching the matrices above.\n\n", tp))
      cor_scatter_panel(w_arm, ASSAYS, color_col = "infant_arm",
                        method = params$cor_method,
                        title = sprintf("Assay-pair scatter by infant arm - %s", tp))
      cat("\n\n")
    } else {
      cat("\n\n*Infant-arm (wP/aP) stratification skipped: no priming variable was resolved.*\n\n")
    }
  }
}

Early pregnancy (maternal)

Method: spearman; pairwise-complete observations; p-values BH-adjusted across the 3 unique assay pairs.

Correlation coefficients (r) – all infants:

r matrix - Early pregnancy (maternal) (all infants)
PRN_IgG FIM_IgG PT_IgG

## tidy long table across all timepoints (for the summary engine / Word reader)
cor_all <- dplyr::bind_rows(lapply(names(cor_results),
                                   function(tp) cor_long(cor_results[[tp]], tp)))
disp <- cor_all %>%
  mutate(r = round(r, 3), p = signif(p, 3), p_BH = signif(p_BH, 3),
         sig = cut(p_BH, c(-Inf, .001, .01, .05, .1, Inf),
                   labels = c("***", "**", "*", ".", ""))) %>%
  arrange(timepoint, desc(abs(r)))
knitr::kable(disp,
  caption = "Table 2. All within-subject assay-pair correlations at each timepoint (sorted by |r| within timepoint).")
Table 2. All within-subject assay-pair correlations at each timepoint (sorted by |r| within timepoint).

## Table 2b: InfMon5/InfMon9 correlations, overall vs stratified by infant arm.
if (length(cor_strata_results)) {
  strata_all <- dplyr::bind_rows(lapply(names(cor_strata_results),
    function(tp) cor_strata_long(cor_strata_results[[tp]], tp)))
  disp_s <- strata_all %>%
    mutate(r = round(r, 3), p = signif(p, 3), p_BH = signif(p_BH, 3),
           sig = cut(p_BH, c(-Inf, .001, .01, .05, .1, Inf),
                     labels = c("***", "**", "*", ".", ""))) %>%
    arrange(timepoint, assay_x, assay_y,
            factor(stratum, levels = c("all", "wP", "aP")))
  print(knitr::kable(disp_s,
    caption = paste0("Table 2b. InfMon5 / InfMon9 within-subject correlations, ",
                     "overall ('all') and stratified by infant arm (wP / aP). ",
                     "BH adjustment is within each (timepoint x stratum) pair set.")))
} else {
  cat("*No infant-arm-stratified correlations were produced ",
      "(no priming variable resolved).*\n\n")
}
Table 2b. InfMon5 / InfMon9 within-subject correlations, overall (‘all’) and stratified by infant arm (wP / aP). BH adjustment is within each (timepoint x stratum) pair set.
timepoint assay_x assay_y r n p p_BH stratum sig
InfMon5 (infant) FIM_IgG PT_IgG -0.018 281 7.68e-01 7.68e-01 all

InfMon9 (infant) WHOLE_WT_IgG PT_IgG -0.016 110 8.71e-01 9.33e-01 aP

How Q1-Q5 should be read

  • Stability of the correlation structure across timepoints tells you whether the six assays measure a single shared “magnitude” axis (all strongly positively correlated) or several semi-independent axes. A pertussis cluster (WHOLE_PTNA, PT_IgG, and possibly WHOLE_WT_IgG) that is tighter than its correlation with FIM_IgG/SBA would indicate antigen-specific structure rather than a single global responder magnitude.
  • Why a within-subject correlation is the right object here. Each row is one subject measured on one draw; correlating the columns asks “do subjects who are high on assay A tend to be high on assay B on the same sample?” This is exactly the input the staged responder-subgrouping step (Q6) needs.
  • The maternal matrices (Q1, Q2) necessarily contain only the three antigen-specific totals (PT_IgG, PRN_IgG, FIM_IgG); the whole-bacterial assays are not measured on maternal serum (Table 1). The full six-assay structure is observable at InfMon2, InfMon5 and InfMon9 (Q3-Q5), letting you watch the structure evolve from the pre-primary baseline through the infant’s own vaccine responses.

Q6 - Staged responder subgroups: maternal -> infant baseline -> infant responses

Strategy

We separate the maternal response from the infant response by building two independent panels, then linking them, so the two signals are never conflated:

  • Stage A (maternal response, up to MatBirth). Binding panel (PT_IgG, FHA_IgG, PRN_IgG) at PregEarly + MatBirth. No whole-bacterial assays exist on maternal serum, so the maternal responder is defined on antigen-specific binding alone. MatBirth is the post-vaccination state; PregEarly the pre-vaccination baseline.
  • Stage B (infant chain). The same binding panel plus the whole-bacterial functional assays (WHOLE_SBA, WHOLE_WT_IgG) across InfMon2 -> InfMon5 -> InfMon9. InfMon2 is the transplacental baseline (what the mother supplied); InfMon5/InfMon9 are the infant’s own post-priming responses.
  • Stage C (linkage). Correlate the maternal and infant scores, cross-tabulate the two subgroupings, and trace the infant score across the three infant visits stratified by maternal subgroup – reading left to right is exactly “maternal response -> infant baseline -> infant responses”.

Each feature is converted to a within-timepoint Blom z-score (so the cross-plate offset cancels), the responder score is the subject mean z over its panel, and a breadth metric separates uniformly high/low (universal) from spiky subjects. Subgroups come from PAM clustering with silhouette-chosen k, with a transparent tertile rule as the fallback/sensitivity check. The reusable engine lives in build_responder_panel(), summarise_responder_subjects(), add_tertile_bands(), build_resp_wide(), detect_subgroups() and responder_score_by_visit().

Stage A - Maternal responder subgroups (up to MatBirth)

cat(sprintf("Maternal panel: features **%s** at **%s** (binding only -- no whole-bacterial on maternal serum).\n\n",
            paste(RESP_FEATS, collapse = ", "), paste(MAT_VIS, collapse = ", ")))

Maternal panel: features PT_IgG, FHA_IgG, PRN_IgG at PregEarly, MatBirth (binding only – no whole-bacterial on maternal serum).

print(knitr::kable(
  mat_long %>% dplyr::count(visit, feature, name = "n_subjects") %>%
    tidyr::pivot_wider(names_from = visit, values_from = n_subjects),
  caption = "Table A1. Maternal responder-feature coverage (subjects) by feature and visit."))
Table A1. Maternal responder-feature coverage (subjects) by feature and visit.
feature MatBirth PregEarly
FHA_IgG 283 312
PRN_IgG 283 312
PT_IgG 283 312 
mat_sg <- stage_report(mat_subject, mat_wide, "Maternal (Stage A)", "A")

Median score by arm: TdaP = 0.361; TT = -0.311. Wilcoxon by arm: W = 18691, p = 2.28e-16.

  • High responders (top tertile & breadth >= 60%): 66; low responders: 67.

PAM silhouette-selected k = 2 (avg widths: 0.291, 0.214, 0.257).

Table A2. Maternal (Stage A) PAM subgroups (labelled by score rank).
subgroup n median_z
High responders 154 0.440
Low responders 129 -0.499
Table A3. Maternal (Stage A) score band x arm (counts).
High score Low score Mid
TdaP 77 21 53
TT 27 83 51

Fisher (simulated p): p = 1e-04.

Stage B - Infant responder subgroups (InfMon2 baseline -> InfMon5/9 responses)

cat(sprintf("Infant panel: binding **%s** + whole-bacterial **%s** at **%s** (%s is the transplacental baseline).\n\n",
            paste(RESP_FEATS, collapse = ", "),
            if (length(RESP_WHOLE)) paste(RESP_WHOLE, collapse = ", ") else "none",
            paste(INF_VIS, collapse = ", "), params$infant_baseline))

Infant panel: binding PT_IgG, FHA_IgG, PRN_IgG + whole-bacterial WHOLE_SBA, WHOLE_WT_IgG at InfMon2, InfMon5, InfMon9 (InfMon2 is the transplacental baseline).

print(knitr::kable(
  inf_long %>% dplyr::count(visit, feature, name = "n_subjects") %>%
    tidyr::pivot_wider(names_from = visit, values_from = n_subjects),
  caption = "Table B1. Infant responder-feature coverage (subjects) by feature and visit."))
Table B1. Infant responder-feature coverage (subjects) by feature and visit.
feature InfMon2 InfMon5 InfMon9
FHA_IgG 287 281 306
PRN_IgG 287 281 306
PT_IgG 287 281 306
WHOLE_SBA 238 239 234
WHOLE_WT_IgG 223 221 222 
inf_sg <- stage_report(inf_subject, inf_wide, "Infant (Stage B)", "B")

Median score by arm: TdaP = 0.061; TT = -0.072. Wilcoxon by arm: W = 14593, p = 0.00152.

  • High responders (top tertile & breadth >= 60%): 32; low responders: 44.

PAM silhouette-selected k = 2 (avg widths: 0.202, 0.121, 0.138).

Table B2. Infant (Stage B) PAM subgroups (labelled by score rank).
subgroup n median_z
High responders 146 0.014
Low responders 118 -0.028
Table B3. Infant (Stage B) score band x arm (counts).
High score Low score Mid
TdaP 64 45 42
TT 40 59 61

Fisher (simulated p): p = 0.006.

Stage B2 - Single-assay responder subgroups

Beyond the multi-assay panel above, we additionally detect responder subgroups on each assay alone – PT_IgG, PRN_IgG, WHOLE_SBA, WHOLE_PTNA, WHOLE_WT_IgG – across the infant visits (InfMon2 -> InfMon5 -> InfMon9). Each single-assay panel is standardised within-timepoint (Blom z), scored as the subject mean z over that assay’s available visits, and clustered with the same PAM/silhouette engine (with min_feat = 1 so a 1-3 column single-assay matrix is actually clustered rather than forced to the tertile fallback). This shows which individual assays carry a clean high/low responder split and how the maternal arm maps onto each. Note the panel spans InfMon2 (transplacental baseline) through the infant’s own responses, so a single-assay “responder” mixes supplied and self-made antibody exactly as the Stage-B panel does.

single_feature_report <- function(sf) {
  if (!isTRUE(sf$ok)) {
    cat(sprintf("\n\n### %s\n\n*Skipped: %s.*\n\n", sf$feature, sf$reason))
    return(invisible(NULL))
  }
  cat(sprintf("\n\n### %s\n\n", sf$feature))
  subj <- sf$subject

  arm_test <- tryCatch(stats::wilcox.test(universal_z ~ arm_name, data = subj),
                       error = function(e) NULL)
  meds <- subj %>% dplyr::group_by(arm_name) %>%
    dplyr::summarize(median_z = round(median(universal_z, na.rm = TRUE), 3),
                     .groups = "drop")
  cat(sprintf("Single-assay score by maternal arm: %s.",
              paste(sprintf("%s = %.3f", meds$arm_name, meds$median_z),
                    collapse = "; ")))
  if (!is.null(arm_test))
    cat(sprintf(" Wilcoxon by arm: **W = %.0f, p = %.3g**.",
                unname(arm_test$statistic), arm_test$p.value))
  cat(sprintf("\n\nUniversal high: **%d**; universal low: **%d**.\n\n",
              sum(subj$universal_high, na.rm = TRUE),
              sum(subj$universal_low,  na.rm = TRUE)))

  sg <- sf$subgroups
  if (isTRUE(sg$ok)) {
    cat(sprintf("PAM silhouette-selected **k = %d** (avg widths: %s).\n\n",
                sg$best_k, paste(round(sg$sil, 3), collapse = ", ")))
    print(knitr::kable(
      sg$subgroup_tbl %>% dplyr::group_by(subgroup) %>%
        dplyr::summarize(n = dplyr::n(),
                         median_z = round(median(universal_z, na.rm = TRUE), 3),
                         .groups = "drop"),
      caption = sprintf("Single-assay PAM subgroups - %s (labelled by score rank).",
                        sf$feature)))
  } else {
    cat(sprintf("*PAM skipped (%s); tertile bands used.*\n\n", sg$reason))
    print(knitr::kable(subj %>% dplyr::count(score_band, name = "n"),
      caption = sprintf("Single-assay tertile bands - %s.", sf$feature)))
  }
  single_feature_dist_plot(sf)
  invisible(sf)
}

single_feature_results <- list()
for (f in params$single_feature_subgroups) {
  sf <- single_feature_subgroup(data_raw, INF_VIS, f, avail_feats, WHOLE_MAP,
                                breadth_thresh = params$breadth_thresh,
                                has_cluster = has_cluster)
  single_feature_results[[f]] <- sf
  single_feature_report(sf)
}

PT_IgG

Single-assay score by maternal arm: TdaP = -0.026; TT = 0.073. Wilcoxon by arm: W = 10934, p = 0.148.

Universal high: 68; universal low: 71.

PAM silhouette-selected k = 2 (avg widths: 0.381, 0.301, 0.297).

Single-assay PAM subgroups - PT_IgG (labelled by score rank).

High responders 110 0.312
Low responders 201 -0.112

Universal high: 77; universal low: 75.

PAM silhouette-selected k = 2 (avg widths: 0.323, 0.216, 0.271).

Single-assay PAM subgroups - PRN_IgG (labelled by score rank).
subgroup n median_z
High responders 172 0.295
Low responders 139 -0.433

WHOLE_SBA

Universal high: 60; universal low: 72.

PAM silhouette-selected k = 2 (avg widths: 0.336, 0.322, 0.303).

Single-assay PAM subgroups - WHOLE_SBA (labelled by score rank).
subgroup n median_z
High responders 97 0.426
Low responders 142 -0.276

WHOLE_PTNA

Universal high: 64; universal low: 59.

PAM silhouette-selected k = 2 (avg widths: 0.331, 0.328, 0.288).

Single-assay PAM subgroups - WHOLE_PTNA (labelled by score rank).
subgroup n median_z
High responders 70 0.541
Low responders 167 -0.227

WHOLE_WT_IgG

Single-assay score by maternal arm: TdaP = 0.039; TT = -0.169. Wilcoxon by arm: W = 7484, p = 0.517.

Universal high: 65; universal low: 59.

PAM silhouette-selected k = 2 (avg widths: 0.356, 0.287, 0.305).

Single-assay PAM subgroups - WHOLE_WT_IgG (labelled by score rank).
subgroup n median_z
High responders 76 0.921
Low responders 163 -0.383

Stage B3 - Cross-assay concordance of the single-assay subgroups

The single-assay subgroups above each split the cohort into Low/High independently. This section asks how much those classifications agree across assays – i.e. whether a subject called High on one antigen tends to be High on the others, or whether the Low/High axis is antigen-specific. We use each subject’s Low/High label from the five single-assay PAM subgroups (collapsed to High/Low), then report pairwise agreement (Cohen’s kappa and raw % agreement, on the subjects each pair classifies in common), the full agreement matrix, and how many assays each subject is classified High on. kappa near 1 = the assays sort subjects the same way; near 0 = independent; negative = systematic disagreement.

sf_lab    <- single_feature_label_table(single_feature_results)
SF_ASSAYS <- intersect(params$single_feature_subgroups, names(sf_lab$wide))

if (length(SF_ASSAYS) >= 2 && nrow(sf_lab$long)) {
  ## (1) pairwise concordance ------------------------------------------------
  pc <- pairwise_concordance(sf_lab$wide, SF_ASSAYS)
  print(knitr::kable(
    pc %>% dplyr::mutate(pct_agree = round(100 * pct_agree, 1),
                         kappa     = round(kappa, 3)) %>%
      dplyr::arrange(dplyr::desc(kappa)),
    caption = paste0("Table B3-1. Pairwise concordance of the single-assay ",
                     "High/Low classifications (Cohen's kappa, % agreement, ",
                     "and n subjects classified by both assays).")))

  ## kappa heatmap
  km  <- kappa_matrix(pc, SF_ASSAYS)
  kdf <- expand.grid(x = SF_ASSAYS, y = SF_ASSAYS,
                     KEEP.OUT.ATTRS = FALSE, stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
  kdf$kappa <- mapply(function(a, b) km[a, b], kdf$x, kdf$y)
  kdf$x <- factor(kdf$x, levels = SF_ASSAYS)
  kdf$y <- factor(kdf$y, levels = rev(SF_ASSAYS))
  print(ggplot(kdf, aes(x, y, fill = kappa)) +
    geom_tile(color = "white") +
    geom_text(aes(label = ifelse(is.na(kappa), "", sprintf("%.2f", kappa))),
              size = 3) +
    scale_fill_gradient2(low = "#d01c8b", mid = "white", high = "#2166ac",
                         midpoint = 0, limits = c(-1, 1), na.value = "grey90",
                         name = "kappa") +
    labs(title = "Cross-assay concordance (Cohen's kappa) of single-assay subgroups",
         x = NULL, y = NULL) +
    theme_bw(base_size = 10) +
    theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)))
  cat("\n\n")

  ## (2) per-subject 'High count' across assays ------------------------------
  hi <- sf_lab$long %>%
    dplyr::group_by(subject_accession) %>%
    dplyr::summarize(n_assays = dplyr::n(),
                     n_high = sum(level == "High"),
                     n_low  = sum(level == "Low"), .groups = "drop")

  full <- hi %>% dplyr::filter(n_assays == length(SF_ASSAYS))
  cat(sprintf(paste0("\n\nAmong the **%d** subjects classified on all %d single-assay ",
                     "panels: **%d** are High on every assay, **%d** are Low on every ",
                     "assay, and **%d** are mixed -- the size of the mixed group is the ",
                     "direct measure of antigen-specific (discordant) responder status.\n\n"),
              nrow(full), length(SF_ASSAYS),
              sum(full$n_high == length(SF_ASSAYS)),
              sum(full$n_low  == length(SF_ASSAYS)),
              sum(full$n_high > 0 & full$n_high < length(SF_ASSAYS))))

  if (nrow(full)) {
    print(ggplot(full, aes(factor(n_high))) +
      geom_bar(fill = "#2166ac") +
      labs(title = sprintf("High-assay count among subjects classified on all %d single-assay panels",
                           length(SF_ASSAYS)),
           x = "Number of assays on which the subject is classified High",
           y = "Subjects") +
      theme_bw(base_size = 10))
    print(knitr::kable(
      full %>% dplyr::count(n_high, name = "n_subjects") %>% dplyr::arrange(n_high),
      caption = "Table B3-2. Distribution of the High-assay count (subjects classified on all single-assay panels)."))
  }
} else {
  cat("*Fewer than two single-assay subgroupings available - no concordance analysis.*\n\n")
  sf_lab <- list(long = dplyr::tibble(), wide = dplyr::tibble())
  hi <- dplyr::tibble(subject_accession = character(),
                      n_assays = integer(), n_high = integer(), n_low = integer())
}
Table B3-1. Pairwise concordance of the single-assay High/Low classifications (Cohen’s kappa, % agreement, and n subjects classified by both assays).
assay_x assay_y n pct_agree kappa
PT_IgG WHOLE_PTNA 237 75.1 0.446
PT_IgG WHOLE_SBA 239 66.9 0.307
WHOLE_SBA WHOLE_PTNA 237 66.2 0.268
PRN_IgG WHOLE_SBA 239 61.9 0.265
PT_IgG PRN_IgG 311 61.4 0.252
WHOLE_SBA WHOLE_WT_IgG 237 59.1 0.120
PRN_IgG WHOLE_PTNA 237 50.6 0.084
PRN_IgG WHOLE_WT_IgG 239 49.8 0.060
WHOLE_PTNA WHOLE_WT_IgG 237 59.9 0.057
PT_IgG WHOLE_WT_IgG 239 50.6 -0.085

Among the 237 subjects classified on all 5 single-assay panels: 10 are High on every assay, 40 are Low on every assay, and 187 are mixed – the size of the mixed group is the direct measure of antigen-specific (discordant) responder status.

Table B3-2. Distribution of the High-assay count (subjects classified on all single-assay panels).
n_high n_subjects
0 40
1 60
2 51
3 45
4 31
5 10

Stage C - Linkage: does the maternal response carry forward?

mat_g <- group_of(mat_subject, mat_sg) %>%
  dplyr::select(subject_accession, maternal_group = group, maternal_z = universal_z)
inf_g <- group_of(inf_subject, inf_sg) %>%
  dplyr::select(subject_accession, infant_group = group, infant_z = universal_z)
link <- dplyr::inner_join(mat_g, inf_g, by = "subject_accession")

## Collapse the detailed subgroup labels to {High, Low, Mid} and form the
## maternal_infant linkage class used by the Stage-D low/low profiling.
link$maternal_level <- collapse_resp_level(link$maternal_group)
link$infant_level   <- collapse_resp_level(link$infant_group)
link$link_class     <- paste(link$maternal_level, link$infant_level, sep = "_")

## (1) maternal score vs infant (pooled) score
cor_mi <- tryCatch(
  stats::cor.test(link$maternal_z, link$infant_z, method = params$cor_method,
                  exact = FALSE), error = function(e) NULL)
cat("### Maternal score vs infant score\n\n")

Maternal score vs infant score

if (!is.null(cor_mi))
  cat(sprintf("Across **%d** linked mother-infant pairs, the maternal responder score and the pooled infant score correlate at **%.2f** (%s, p = %.3g).\n\n",
              nrow(link), unname(cor_mi$estimate), params$cor_method, cor_mi$p.value))

Across 311 linked mother-infant pairs, the maternal responder score and the pooled infant score correlate at 0.17 (spearman, p = 0.0025).

## (2) maternal subgroup x infant subgroup
cat("### Maternal subgroup x infant subgroup\n\n")

Maternal subgroup x infant subgroup

tab_mi <- table(link$maternal_group, link$infant_group)
ft_mi  <- tryCatch(stats::fisher.test(tab_mi, simulate.p.value = TRUE, B = 1e4),
                   error = function(e) NULL)
print(knitr::kable(as.data.frame.matrix(tab_mi),
      caption = "Table C1. Maternal subgroup (rows) x infant subgroup (cols), linked pairs."))
Table C1. Maternal subgroup (rows) x infant subgroup (cols), linked pairs.
High responders High score Low responders Low score Mid
High responders 36 9 87 17 4
High score 3 0 8 0 0
Low responders 98 3 14 11 3
Low score 4 0 4 0 0
Mid 5 0 5 0 0 
if (!is.null(ft_mi)) cat(sprintf("\nFisher (simulated p): **p = %.3g**.\n\n", ft_mi$p.value))

Fisher (simulated p): p = 1e-04.

## (3) infant trajectory across visits, stratified by MATERNAL subgroup:
##     baseline (InfMon2) -> responses (InfMon5, InfMon9)
cat("### Infant trajectory by maternal subgroup (baseline -> responses)\n\n")

Infant trajectory by maternal subgroup (baseline -> responses)

traj <- inf_by_visit %>%
  dplyr::left_join(dplyr::select(mat_g, subject_accession, maternal_group),
                   by = "subject_accession") %>%
  dplyr::filter(!is.na(maternal_group), visit %in% INF_VIS) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(visit = factor(visit, levels = INF_VIS)) %>%
  dplyr::group_by(maternal_group, visit) %>%
  dplyr::summarize(mean_z = mean(score_visit, na.rm = TRUE),
                   se = stats::sd(score_visit, na.rm = TRUE) /
                        sqrt(sum(is.finite(score_visit))),
                   n = dplyr::n(), .groups = "drop")

if (nrow(traj)) {
  print(ggplot(traj, aes(visit, mean_z, color = maternal_group, group = maternal_group)) +
    geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) + geom_point(size = 2) +
    geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = mean_z - se, ymax = mean_z + se), width = 0.12) +
    labs(x = NULL, y = "Infant responder score (mean z +/- SE)",
         color = "Maternal subgroup",
         title = "Infant trajectory by maternal subgroup",
         subtitle = sprintf("%s (baseline) -> %s",
                            params$infant_baseline,
                            paste(setdiff(INF_VIS, params$infant_baseline), collapse = " -> "))) +
    theme_bw(base_size = 11))
  print(knitr::kable(
    traj %>% dplyr::mutate(mean_z = round(mean_z, 3), se = round(se, 3)),
    caption = "Table C2. Infant score by maternal subgroup and infant visit (mean z, SE, n)."))
}

Table C2. Infant score by maternal subgroup and infant visit (mean z, SE, n).
maternal_group visit mean_z se n
High responders InfMon2 0.510 0.053 136
High responders InfMon5 -0.206 0.049 134
High responders InfMon9 -0.214 0.053 151
High score InfMon2 0.675 0.249 11
High score InfMon5 -0.089 0.160 11
High score InfMon9 -0.181 0.200 11
Low responders InfMon2 -0.624 0.057 122
Low responders InfMon5 0.203 0.052 118
Low responders InfMon9 0.256 0.056 127
Low score InfMon2 -0.111 0.202 8
Low score InfMon5 -0.184 0.164 8
Low score InfMon9 -0.157 0.155 7
Mid InfMon2 0.194 0.231 10
Mid InfMon5 0.046 0.126 10
Mid InfMon9 -0.177 0.162 10

Stage D - Profiling the low/low subjects

The Stage-C verdict flags the low-into-low pairs – subjects who are low maternal responders and low infant responders – as the consistent non-responders. Here we identify those individuals, profile their serology across the full standardised panel (maternal binding at PregEarly/MatBirth plus the infant binding/functional assays at InfMon2/5/9), and contrast that profile with the other linkage classes to ask what is different about them.

## Combined standardised profile panel (maternal + infant feature@visit z).
prof_long <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  dplyr::mutate(mat_long, panel = "maternal"),
  dplyr::mutate(inf_long, panel = "infant"))

## Per-subject class + arms.
cls <- link %>%
  dplyr::select(subject_accession, link_class, maternal_level, infant_level)
cls$maternal_arm <- mat_subject$arm_name[
  match(cls$subject_accession, mat_subject$subject_accession)]
cls <- attach_infant_arm(cls, infant_arm_tbl)

## (D1) collapsed class grid + low/low count -------------------------------
cat("### Collapsed maternal x infant linkage classes\n\n")

Collapsed maternal x infant linkage classes

tab_lvl <- table(link$maternal_level, link$infant_level)
print(knitr::kable(as.data.frame.matrix(tab_lvl),
      caption = "Table D1. Collapsed maternal (rows) x infant (cols) responder levels."))
Table D1. Collapsed maternal (rows) x infant (cols) responder levels.
High Low Mid
High 48 112 4
Low 105 29 3
Mid 5 5 0 
n_ll <- sum(link$link_class == "Low_Low", na.rm = TRUE)
cat(sprintf("\n\n**Low/low subjects (maternal Low AND infant Low): n = %d.**\n\n", n_ll))

Low/low subjects (maternal Low AND infant Low): n = 29.

## (D2) the low/low roster -------------------------------------------------
roster <- cls %>%
  dplyr::filter(link_class == "Low_Low") %>%
  dplyr::left_join(dplyr::select(link, subject_accession, maternal_z, infant_z),
                   by = "subject_accession") %>%
  dplyr::transmute(subject_accession, maternal_arm, infant_arm,
                   maternal_z = round(maternal_z, 2),
                   infant_z   = round(infant_z, 2)) %>%
  dplyr::arrange(infant_z)
if (nrow(roster))
  print(knitr::kable(roster,
        caption = "Table D2. The low/low subjects: arms and responder scores."))
Table D2. The low/low subjects: arms and responder scores.
subject_accession maternal_arm infant_arm maternal_z infant_z
103 TT aP -1.72 -1.18
150 TT wP -0.61 -1.01
28 TdaP wP -0.19 -0.91
99 TT wP -0.34 -0.73
110 TT aP -0.99 -0.73
113 TT aP -1.91 -0.57
126 TT aP -1.13 -0.51
231 TdaP aP -0.31 -0.50
114 TT aP -0.66 -0.49
118 TT wP -0.45 -0.40
144 TT wP -0.09 -0.38
634 TdaP wP -0.35 -0.37
143 TT wP -0.18 -0.36
25 TdaP wP -0.12 -0.31
124 TT aP -0.17 -0.27
98 TT aP -0.66 -0.25
606 TdaP aP -1.22 -0.20
161 TdaP aP -1.25 -0.19
178 TdaP wP -0.23 -0.11
644 TT wP -0.06 -0.11
608 TdaP wP -1.17 -0.06
79 TdaP wP -0.25 0.08
633 TdaP wP -0.30 0.12
171 TdaP wP -0.66 0.13
689 TdaP wP -0.46 0.15
47 TT wP -0.49 0.41
61 TdaP aP -0.37 0.41
40 TdaP aP -0.50 0.42
93 TdaP aP -0.47 0.48 
## (D3) serological profile by class (assay-level, averaged over visits) ----
prof_long$assay <- sub("@.*$", "", prof_long$key)
cls_min   <- dplyr::distinct(dplyr::select(cls, subject_accession, link_class))
prof_assay <- prof_long %>%
  dplyr::left_join(cls_min, by = "subject_accession") %>%
  dplyr::filter(!is.na(link_class)) %>%
  dplyr::group_by(link_class, assay) %>%
  dplyr::summarize(mean_z = mean(z, na.rm = TRUE),
                   n = sum(is.finite(z)), .groups = "drop")

if (nrow(prof_assay)) {
  print(ggplot(prof_assay, aes(assay, link_class, fill = mean_z)) +
    geom_tile(color = "white") +
    geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%.2f", mean_z)), size = 2.6) +
    scale_fill_gradient2(low = "#d01c8b", mid = "white", high = "#2166ac",
                         midpoint = 0, name = "mean z") +
    labs(title = "Serological profile by linkage class",
         subtitle = "mean within-timepoint z, averaged over visits",
         x = NULL, y = NULL) +
    theme_bw(base_size = 10) +
    theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)))
  print(knitr::kable(
    prof_assay %>% dplyr::select(link_class, assay, mean_z) %>%
      dplyr::mutate(mean_z = round(mean_z, 3)) %>%
      tidyr::pivot_wider(names_from = assay, values_from = mean_z,
                         id_cols = link_class),
    caption = "Table D3. Mean within-timepoint z by linkage class and assay (averaged over visits)."))
}

Table D3. Mean within-timepoint z by linkage class and assay (averaged over visits).
link_class FHA_IgG PRN_IgG PT_IgG WHOLE_SBA WHOLE_WT_IgG
High_High 0.503 0.345 0.243 -0.087 -0.168
High_Low 0.256 0.262 0.133 0.101 0.016
High_Mid 0.238 0.396 0.521 NA NA
Low_High -0.404 -0.293 -0.184 0.088 -0.015
Low_Low -0.432 -0.503 -0.319 -0.031 0.595
Low_Mid -0.306 -0.035 -0.201 NA NA
Mid_High 0.169 -0.100 0.193 -0.369 -0.360
Mid_Low 0.461 -0.086 0.051 -0.144 0.056 
## (D4) which feature@visit keys most distinguish the low/low subjects ------
dx <- distinguishing_features(prof_long, link, focal = "Low_Low")
if (nrow(dx)) {
  print(knitr::kable(
    utils::head(dx, 12) %>%
      dplyr::transmute(key, n_focal, n_other,
                       mean_focal = round(mean_focal, 2),
                       mean_other = round(mean_other, 2),
                       diff = round(diff, 2),
                       p = signif(p, 3), p_BH = signif(p_BH, 3)),
    caption = paste0("Table D4. feature@visit keys where low/low subjects ",
                     "deviate most from the rest (most negative diff first); ",
                     "Wilcoxon low/low vs rest, BH-adjusted.")))
  top_neg <- utils::head(dx$key[order(dx$diff)], 5)
  cat(sprintf("\n\nThe low/low subjects sit furthest below the rest of the cohort on: **%s**.\n\n",
              paste(top_neg, collapse = ", ")))
}
Table D4. feature@visit keys where low/low subjects deviate most from the rest (most negative diff first); Wilcoxon low/low vs rest, BH-adjusted.
key n_focal n_other mean_focal mean_other diff p p_BH
1 FHA_IgG@PregEarly 29 282 -0.78 0.08 -0.86 1.68e-05 0.000353
13 PRN_IgG@InfMon5 21 260 -0.71 0.06 -0.77 4.02e-04 0.002750
2 PRN_IgG@PregEarly 29 282 -0.66 0.07 -0.73 6.60e-05 0.000693
5 PRN_IgG@MatBirth 25 257 -0.63 0.07 -0.70 5.24e-04 0.002750
14 PT_IgG@InfMon5 21 260 -0.60 0.05 -0.64 3.74e-03 0.015700
4 FHA_IgG@MatBirth 25 257 -0.51 0.05 -0.56 1.41e-02 0.042200
3 PT_IgG@PregEarly 29 282 -0.45 0.04 -0.49 4.64e-03 0.016200
19 PT_IgG@InfMon9 28 278 -0.41 0.04 -0.45 3.24e-02 0.061900
12 FHA_IgG@InfMon5 21 260 -0.40 0.03 -0.43 3.77e-02 0.065900
17 FHA_IgG@InfMon9 28 278 -0.36 0.04 -0.40 5.64e-02 0.091100
18 PRN_IgG@InfMon9 28 278 -0.34 0.03 -0.38 8.38e-02 0.117000
6 PT_IgG@MatBirth 25 257 -0.31 0.03 -0.34 7.54e-02 0.113000

The low/low subjects sit furthest below the rest of the cohort on: FHA_IgG@PregEarly, PRN_IgG@InfMon5, PRN_IgG@PregEarly, PRN_IgG@MatBirth, PT_IgG@InfMon5.

## (D5) arm composition of the low/low subjects ----------------------------
cls$is_ll <- ifelse(cls$link_class == "Low_Low", "Low/low", "Other")
cat("### Arm composition of the low/low subjects\n\n")

Arm composition of the low/low subjects

print(knitr::kable(as.data.frame.matrix(table(cls$is_ll, cls$maternal_arm)),
      caption = "Table D5. Maternal arm (TdaP / TT) by low/low vs other."))
Table D5. Maternal arm (TdaP / TT) by low/low vs other.
TdaP TT
Low/low 15 14
Other 136 146 
ft_arm <- tryCatch(stats::fisher.test(table(cls$is_ll, cls$maternal_arm)),
                   error = function(e) NULL)
if (!is.null(ft_arm))
  cat(sprintf("\nFisher (maternal arm x low/low): **p = %.3g**.\n\n", ft_arm$p.value))

Fisher (maternal arm x low/low): p = 0.846.

if (!is.null(infant_arm_tbl) && any(!is.na(cls$infant_arm))) {
  print(knitr::kable(as.data.frame.matrix(table(cls$is_ll, cls$infant_arm)),
        caption = "Table D6. Infant arm (wP / aP) by low/low vs other."))
  ft_inf <- tryCatch(stats::fisher.test(table(cls$is_ll, cls$infant_arm)),
                     error = function(e) NULL)
  if (!is.null(ft_inf))
    cat(sprintf("\nFisher (infant arm x low/low): **p = %.3g**.\n\n", ft_inf$p.value))
}
Table D6. Infant arm (wP / aP) by low/low vs other.
wP aP
Low/low 16 13
Other 140 142

Fisher (infant arm x low/low): p = 0.697.

Low/low decomposed across the single-assay subgroups

The combined maternal and infant scores compress many assays into one number, which makes a “low/low” label hard to read mechanistically. Here we map each low/low subject onto the Stage-B3 single-assay classifications, so the combined label is decomposed into which individual assay signals are Low or High for these subjects – showing the relative contribution of each antigen/analyte.

ll_ids <- link$subject_accession[which(link$link_class == "Low_Low")]
cat(sprintf("Low/low subjects: **n = %d**.\n\n", length(ll_ids)))

Low/low subjects: n = 29.

if (length(ll_ids) && nrow(sf_lab$long)) {
  ll_lab <- sf_lab$long %>% dplyr::filter(subject_accession %in% ll_ids)

  if (nrow(ll_lab)) {
    ll_lab$assay <- factor(ll_lab$assay, levels = SF_ASSAYS)
    ll_lab$level <- factor(ll_lab$level, levels = c("Low", "High"))

    ## (1) per-subject x assay map of Low/High for the low/low subjects
    print(ggplot(ll_lab,
                 aes(assay, factor(subject_accession), fill = level)) +
      geom_tile(color = "white") +
      scale_fill_manual(values = c(Low = "#d01c8b", High = "#2166ac"),
                        drop = FALSE, name = "Single-assay class") +
      labs(title = "Low/low subjects: single-assay High/Low classification",
           x = NULL, y = "Subject (accession)") +
      theme_bw(base_size = 9) +
      theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)))
    cat("\n\n")

    ## (2) per-assay: % of low/low classified Low vs the cohort baseline
    base_rate <- sf_lab$long %>% dplyr::group_by(assay) %>%
      dplyr::summarize(cohort_pct_low = 100 * mean(level == "Low"),
                       .groups = "drop")
    ll_rate <- ll_lab %>% dplyr::group_by(assay) %>%
      dplyr::summarize(n_ll = dplyr::n(),
                       ll_pct_low = 100 * mean(level == "Low"), .groups = "drop")
    cmp <- ll_rate %>%
      dplyr::left_join(base_rate, by = "assay") %>%
      dplyr::mutate(ll_pct_low     = round(ll_pct_low, 1),
                    cohort_pct_low = round(cohort_pct_low, 1),
                    enrichment     = round(ll_pct_low - cohort_pct_low, 1)) %>%
      dplyr::arrange(dplyr::desc(ll_pct_low))
    print(knitr::kable(cmp,
      caption = paste0("Table D7. For each single-assay subgroup: the % of low/low ",
                       "subjects (n_ll classified on that assay) called Low, the ",
                       "cohort baseline % Low, and the enrichment (difference). ",
                       "Higher % Low = that assay signal contributes more to the ",
                       "low/low phenotype.")))

    ## (3) how concordant is the low/low low-ness across assays?
    ll_hi <- hi %>% dplyr::filter(subject_accession %in% ll_ids)
    if (nrow(ll_hi)) {
      cat(sprintf(paste0("\n\nAcross the single-assay panels, low/low subjects are ",
                         "classified **Low** on a median of **%g** of the assays they ",
                         "were scored on (range %d-%d); **%d of %d** are Low on *every* ",
                         "assay they were classified on, versus High on at least one for ",
                         "the remaining %d.\n\n"),
                  stats::median(ll_hi$n_low, na.rm = TRUE),
                  min(ll_hi$n_low), max(ll_hi$n_low),
                  sum(ll_hi$n_low == ll_hi$n_assays), nrow(ll_hi),
                  sum(ll_hi$n_low <  ll_hi$n_assays)))
      cat(sprintf(paste0("The assay signals contributing most consistently to the ",
                         "low/low phenotype (highest %% Low) are, in order: **%s**. ",
                         "Assays where low/low subjects are *not* uniformly Low identify ",
                         "where the combined label is driven by other antigens rather ",
                         "than a global deficit.\n\n"),
                  paste(as.character(cmp$assay), collapse = ", ")))
    }
  } else {
    cat("*No low/low subjects were classified by any single-assay subgroup.*\n\n")
  }
} else {
  cat("*No low/low subjects or no single-assay labels available to map.*\n\n")
}

Table D7. For each single-assay subgroup: the % of low/low subjects (n_ll classified on that assay) called Low, the cohort baseline % Low, and the enrichment (difference). Higher % Low = that assay signal contributes more to the low/low phenotype.
assay n_ll ll_pct_low cohort_pct_low enrichment
PT_IgG 29 75.9 64.6 11.3
WHOLE_PTNA 14 71.4 70.5 0.9
WHOLE_SBA 15 66.7 59.4 7.3
PRN_IgG 29 65.5 44.7 20.8
WHOLE_WT_IgG 14 28.6 68.2 -39.6

Across the single-assay panels, low/low subjects are classified Low on a median of 2 of the assays they were scored on (range 0-5); 8 of 29 are Low on every assay they were classified on, versus High on at least one for the remaining 21.

The assay signals contributing most consistently to the low/low phenotype (highest % Low) are, in order: PT_IgG, WHOLE_PTNA, WHOLE_SBA, PRN_IgG, WHOLE_WT_IgG. Assays where low/low subjects are not uniformly Low identify where the combined label is driven by other antigens rather than a global deficit.

Q6 (extended) - Pan-assay responder classes and the low/low contrast

Stage E - Always-high / always-low / mixed classes from the concordance

The Stage-B3 high-assay count partitions the subjects who were classified on all single-assay panels into three pan-assay classes: always-high (High on every assay), always-low (Low on every assay), and the mixed middle (High on some, Low on others). Here we give each class a serological profile across the full standardised panel and trace the infant trajectory, then (next subsection) contrast the concordance-defined always-low group with the linkage-defined low/low group, since the two are different constructions of “low”.

n_sf <- length(SF_ASSAYS)
conc <- hi %>%
  dplyr::filter(n_assays == n_sf) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(conc_class = dplyr::case_when(
    n_high == n_sf ~ "Always-high",
    n_low  == n_sf ~ "Always-low",
    TRUE           ~ "Mixed-middle"))
CC_LEVELS <- c("Always-low", "Mixed-middle", "Always-high")
conc$conc_class <- factor(conc$conc_class, levels = CC_LEVELS)

cat(sprintf(paste0("Defined on the **%d** subjects classified on all %d single-assay ",
                   "panels: **%d** always-high, **%d** mixed-middle, **%d** always-low.\n\n"),
            nrow(conc), n_sf,
            sum(conc$conc_class == "Always-high"),
            sum(conc$conc_class == "Mixed-middle"),
            sum(conc$conc_class == "Always-low")))

Defined on the 237 subjects classified on all 5 single-assay panels: 10 always-high, 187 mixed-middle, 40 always-low.

print(knitr::kable(conc %>% dplyr::count(conc_class, name = "n_subjects"),
      caption = "Table E1. Pan-assay concordance classes (subjects classified on all single-assay panels)."))
Table E1. Pan-assay concordance classes (subjects classified on all single-assay panels).
conc_class n_subjects
Always-low 40
Mixed-middle 187
Always-high 10 
## (E2) serological profile across the full panel (assay-level, over visits)
prof_conc <- assay_profile_table(prof_long, conc, "conc_class")
print(profile_heatmap(prof_conc,
      "Serological profile by pan-assay concordance class (mean z, averaged over visits)",
      grp_levels = CC_LEVELS))

print(knitr::kable(
  prof_conc %>% dplyr::mutate(mean_z = round(mean_z, 3)) %>%
    dplyr::select(grp, assay, mean_z) %>%
    tidyr::pivot_wider(names_from = assay, values_from = mean_z, id_cols = grp),
  caption = "Table E2. Mean within-timepoint z by concordance class and assay (averaged over visits)."))
Table E2. Mean within-timepoint z by concordance class and assay (averaged over visits).
grp FHA_IgG PRN_IgG PT_IgG WHOLE_SBA WHOLE_WT_IgG
Always-low 0.205 -0.003 -0.057 -0.272 -0.550
Mixed-middle 0.048 0.057 0.009 0.074 0.062
Always-high -0.477 -0.171 -0.056 0.725 0.995 
## (E3) infant responder trajectory by class
traj_cc <- inf_by_visit %>%
  dplyr::left_join(dplyr::select(conc, subject_accession, conc_class),
                   by = "subject_accession") %>%
  dplyr::filter(!is.na(conc_class), visit %in% INF_VIS) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(visit = factor(visit, levels = INF_VIS)) %>%
  dplyr::group_by(conc_class, visit) %>%
  dplyr::summarize(mean_z = mean(score_visit, na.rm = TRUE),
                   se = stats::sd(score_visit, na.rm = TRUE) /
                        sqrt(sum(is.finite(score_visit))),
                   n = dplyr::n(), .groups = "drop")
if (nrow(traj_cc))
  print(ggplot(traj_cc, aes(visit, mean_z, color = conc_class, group = conc_class)) +
    geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) + geom_point(size = 2) +
    geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = mean_z - se, ymax = mean_z + se), width = 0.12) +
    scale_color_manual(values = c("Always-low" = "#d01c8b",
                                  "Mixed-middle" = "grey50",
                                  "Always-high" = "#2166ac"), name = "Class") +
    labs(x = NULL, y = "Infant responder score (mean z +/- SE)",
         title = "Infant trajectory by pan-assay concordance class") +
    theme_bw(base_size = 11))

## (E4) arm composition
conc2 <- conc %>% dplyr::left_join(
  dplyr::select(cls, subject_accession, maternal_arm, infant_arm),
  by = "subject_accession")
print(knitr::kable(as.data.frame.matrix(table(conc2$conc_class, conc2$maternal_arm)),
      caption = "Table E3. Maternal arm (TT / TdaP) by concordance class."))
Table E3. Maternal arm (TT / TdaP) by concordance class.
TdaP TT
Always-low 31 9
Mixed-middle 85 102
Always-high 0 10 
if (!is.null(infant_arm_tbl) && any(!is.na(conc2$infant_arm)))
  print(knitr::kable(as.data.frame.matrix(table(conc2$conc_class, conc2$infant_arm)),
        caption = "Table E4. Infant arm (wP / aP) by concordance class."))
Table E4. Infant arm (wP / aP) by concordance class.
wP aP
Always-low 26 14
Mixed-middle 87 100
Always-high 7 3

Stage E2 - Low/low vs always-low: two different “low” definitions

low_low (Stage D) means low on the combined maternal score and the combined infant score; always-low (Stage E) means Low on every single-assay infant panel. They answer different questions, so we examine their overlap and what separates them.

ll_ids <- as.character(link$subject_accession[which(link$link_class == "Low_Low")])
al_ids <- as.character(conc$subject_accession[conc$conc_class == "Always-low"])

cat(sprintf("### Low/low (n = %d) vs always-low (n = %d)\n\n",
            length(ll_ids), length(al_ids)))

Low/low (n = 29) vs always-low (n = 40)

## (E5) overlap / membership
both <- intersect(ll_ids, al_ids)
cat(sprintf(paste0("Overlap: **%d** subjects are in both. Of the %d low/low, **%d** are ",
                   "also always-low; of the %d always-low, **%d** are also low/low ",
                   "(so %d always-low subjects are *not* low/low).\n\n"),
            length(both), length(ll_ids), length(both),
            length(al_ids), length(both), length(al_ids) - length(both)))

Overlap: 3 subjects are in both. Of the 29 low/low, 3 are also always-low; of the 40 always-low, 3 are also low/low (so 37 always-low subjects are not low/low).

uni  <- union(ll_ids, al_ids)
memb <- data.frame(
  low_low    = ifelse(uni %in% ll_ids, "low_low", "not"),
  always_low = ifelse(uni %in% al_ids, "always_low", "not"),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
print(knitr::kable(as.data.frame.matrix(table(memb$low_low, memb$always_low)),
      caption = "Table E5. Membership overlap: low/low (rows) x always-low (cols), over the union of the two groups."))
Table E5. Membership overlap: low/low (rows) x always-low (cols), over the union of the two groups.
always_low not
low_low 3 26
not 37 0 
## (E6) side-by-side serological profile, by feature@visit (key level)
m_ll <- prof_long %>% dplyr::filter(subject_accession %in% ll_ids) %>%
  dplyr::group_by(key) %>% dplyr::summarize(low_low = mean(z, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")
m_al <- prof_long %>% dplyr::filter(subject_accession %in% al_ids) %>%
  dplyr::group_by(key) %>% dplyr::summarize(always_low = mean(z, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")
diff_key <- dplyr::full_join(m_ll, m_al, by = "key") %>%
  dplyr::mutate(diff = always_low - low_low)
diff_key$visit <- sub("^.*@", "", diff_key$key)
diff_key$assay <- sub("@.*$", "", diff_key$key)

## heatmap (two rows) ordered by visit then assay
vord <- c("PregEarly", "MatBirth", "InfMon2", "InfMon5", "InfMon9")
ko   <- diff_key[order(match(diff_key$visit, vord), diff_key$assay), ]
two_hm <- dplyr::bind_rows(
  data.frame(grp = sprintf("Low_low (n=%d)", length(ll_ids)),
             assay = ko$key, mean_z = ko$low_low, stringsAsFactors = FALSE),
  data.frame(grp = sprintf("Always-low (n=%d)", length(al_ids)),
             assay = ko$key, mean_z = ko$always_low, stringsAsFactors = FALSE))
two_hm$assay <- factor(two_hm$assay, levels = ko$key)
grp_lv <- c(sprintf("Low_low (n=%d)", length(ll_ids)),
            sprintf("Always-low (n=%d)", length(al_ids)))
print(profile_heatmap(two_hm,
      "Low/low vs always-low: serological profile by feature@visit (mean z)",
      grp_levels = grp_lv))

diff_disp <- diff_key %>%
  dplyr::mutate(low_low = round(low_low, 2), always_low = round(always_low, 2),
                diff = round(diff, 2)) %>%
  dplyr::arrange(dplyr::desc(abs(diff))) %>%
  dplyr::select(key, low_low, always_low, diff)
print(knitr::kable(utils::head(diff_disp, 14),
      caption = paste0("Table E6. Mean z per feature@visit for low/low vs always-low, ",
                       "and the difference (always-low minus low/low), sorted by ",
                       "|difference|. Positive = always-low sits higher.")))
Table E6. Mean z per feature@visit for low/low vs always-low, and the difference (always-low minus low/low), sorted by |difference|. Positive = always-low sits higher.
key low_low always_low diff
WHOLE_WT_IgG@InfMon5 0.68 -0.87 -1.54
WHOLE_WT_IgG@InfMon9 0.56 -0.83 -1.39
PRN_IgG@MatBirth -0.63 0.59 1.23
FHA_IgG@MatBirth -0.51 0.60 1.10
FHA_IgG@PregEarly -0.78 0.29 1.08
PRN_IgG@PregEarly -0.66 0.40 1.05
PT_IgG@MatBirth -0.31 0.62 0.93
PRN_IgG@InfMon2 -0.21 0.56 0.77
WHOLE_SBA@InfMon5 -0.21 -0.84 -0.63
FHA_IgG@InfMon2 -0.07 0.54 0.61
PT_IgG@PregEarly -0.45 0.09 0.54
WHOLE_WT_IgG@InfMon2 0.57 0.05 -0.51
PT_IgG@InfMon2 0.13 0.60 0.47
PRN_IgG@InfMon9 -0.34 -0.74 -0.40 
## (E7) arm mix + combined scores
mat_arm_of <- function(ids) mat_subject$arm_name[match(ids, mat_subject$subject_accession)]
grp_row <- function(ids, label) {
  d <- link %>% dplyr::filter(subject_accession %in% ids)
  data.frame(group = label, n = length(ids),
             pct_TT_mothers = round(100 * mean(mat_arm_of(ids) == "TT", na.rm = TRUE), 0),
             mean_maternal_z = round(mean(d$maternal_z, na.rm = TRUE), 2),
             mean_infant_z   = round(mean(d$infant_z,   na.rm = TRUE), 2),
             stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
}
cmp_tab <- dplyr::bind_rows(grp_row(ll_ids, "Low_low"),
                            grp_row(al_ids, "Always-low"))
print(knitr::kable(cmp_tab,
      caption = "Table E7. Low/low vs always-low: % TT mothers, and mean combined maternal / infant scores."))
Table E7. Low/low vs always-low: % TT mothers, and mean combined maternal / infant scores.
group n pct_TT_mothers mean_maternal_z mean_infant_z
Low_low 29 48 -0.6 -0.26
Always-low 40 22 0.4 -0.27 
cat(sprintf(paste0(
  "\n\n**Interpretation.** Always-low is defined purely on the infant's own responses ",
  "(Low on all %d single-assay panels); low/low additionally requires a *low maternal* ",
  "responder. They overlap in %d subjects but are not the same: always-low also contains ",
  "infants of strong-responder mothers (the blunted high-maternal -> low-infant case), so ",
  "the always-low group should carry a **higher maternal-visit signal** (look for positive ",
  "differences on the PregEarly / MatBirth PT_IgG and PRN_IgG keys in Table E6) and a larger ",
  "TdaP share (Table E7) than low/low. Low/low subjects lack the maternal head-start: they ",
  "are low on both the maternal-visit antigens *and* the infant responses -- a global deficit ",
  "rather than a blunting of transferred antibody. In short, the two groups agree on the ",
  "infant being low, but differ on **why**: always-low mixes 'never made it' with 'had it and ",
  "lost it', while low/low isolates 'never had it'.\n\n"),
  n_sf, length(both)))

Interpretation. Always-low is defined purely on the infant’s own responses (Low on all 5 single-assay panels); low/low additionally requires a low maternal responder. They overlap in 3 subjects but are not the same: always-low also contains infants of strong-responder mothers (the blunted high-maternal -> low-infant case), so the always-low group should carry a higher maternal-visit signal (look for positive differences on the PregEarly / MatBirth PT_IgG and PRN_IgG keys in Table E6) and a larger TdaP share (Table E7) than low/low. Low/low subjects lack the maternal head-start: they are low on both the maternal-visit antigens and the infant responses – a global deficit rather than a blunting of transferred antibody. In short, the two groups agree on the infant being low, but differ on why: always-low mixes ‘never made it’ with ‘had it and lost it’, while low/low isolates ‘never had it’.

n_hi_mat <- sum(mat_subject$universal_high, na.rm = TRUE)
n_lo_mat <- sum(mat_subject$universal_low,  na.rm = TRUE)
n_hi_inf <- sum(inf_subject$universal_high, na.rm = TRUE)
n_lo_inf <- sum(inf_subject$universal_low,  na.rm = TRUE)

cat("## Verdict on Q6\n\n")

Verdict on Q6

cat(sprintf(paste0(
  "- **Stage A (maternal):** %d high and %d low universal maternal responders up to MatBirth.\n",
  "- **Stage B (infant):** %d high and %d low universal infant responders across %s.\n",
  "- **Stage C (linkage):** read Table C1 with the maternal-vs-infant score correlation and the trajectory plot. ",
  "A positive correlation with high-maternal subgroups starting high at the **%s** baseline is the transplacental signal; ",
  "whether the curves stay separated or converge at InfMon5/InfMon9 shows how much the maternal advantage persists once the infant's own priming takes over. ",
  "Low infant responders whose mothers were high responders are the loss-of-transfer cases to flag; low-into-low pairs are the consistent non-responders.\n\n"),
  n_hi_mat, n_lo_mat, n_hi_inf, n_lo_inf,
  paste(INF_VIS, collapse = " -> "), params$infant_baseline))
  • Stage A (maternal): 66 high and 67 low universal maternal responders up to MatBirth.
  • Stage B (infant): 32 high and 44 low universal infant responders across InfMon2 -> InfMon5 -> InfMon9.
  • Stage C (linkage): read Table C1 with the maternal-vs-infant score correlation and the trajectory plot. A positive correlation with high-maternal subgroups starting high at the InfMon2 baseline is the transplacental signal; whether the curves stay separated or converge at InfMon5/InfMon9 shows how much the maternal advantage persists once the infant’s own priming takes over. Low infant responders whose mothers were high responders are the loss-of-transfer cases to flag; low-into-low pairs are the consistent non-responders.
cat(sprintf(paste0(
  "- **Stage B2 (single-assay):** responder subgroups detected separately on %s.\n",
  "- **Stage B3 (concordance):** Table B3-1 / the kappa heatmap show how far those ",
  "single-assay Low/High calls agree across antigens; the mixed-count is the antigen-specific share.\n",
  "- **Stage D (low/low profile):** %d subjects are low on both axes; Tables D3-D6 ",
  "show how their serological profile and arm composition differ, and Table D7 decomposes ",
  "the combined low/low label into the per-assay signals that drive it.\n",
  "- **Stage E (pan-assay classes):** always-high / always-low / mixed classes are profiled, ",
  "and the concordance-defined always-low group is contrasted with the linkage-defined low/low ",
  "group (Tables E5-E7) -- the two 'low' constructions overlap but differ in maternal contribution.\n\n"),
  paste(params$single_feature_subgroups, collapse = ", "),
  sum(link$link_class == "Low_Low", na.rm = TRUE)))
  • Stage B2 (single-assay): responder subgroups detected separately on PT_IgG, PRN_IgG, WHOLE_SBA, WHOLE_PTNA, WHOLE_WT_IgG.
  • Stage B3 (concordance): Table B3-1 / the kappa heatmap show how far those single-assay Low/High calls agree across antigens; the mixed-count is the antigen-specific share.
  • Stage D (low/low profile): 29 subjects are low on both axes; Tables D3-D6 show how their serological profile and arm composition differ, and Table D7 decomposes the combined low/low label into the per-assay signals that drive it.
  • Stage E (pan-assay classes): always-high / always-low / mixed classes are profiled, and the concordance-defined always-low group is contrasted with the linkage-defined low/low group (Tables E5-E7) – the two ‘low’ constructions overlap but differ in maternal contribution.

Global Participant Accounting

nt_render(nt, caption = "Participant accounting across this analysis.")
